title: Southeast Asia & Oceania
subtitle: Temple cities, island stone, and the deepest-time painting tradition on earth.
intro: From the sandstone temple-mountains of Angkor and Borobudur to basalt moai on Easter Island and rock shelters in northern Australia where painting has gone on for 20,000 years.

works.title: Borobudur
works.desc: A stone mandala you walk. Climbing its nine terraces takes you through 2,672 relief panels and 504 Buddhas to the empty stupa at the top.

works.title: Prambanan
works.desc: The Hindu counterpart to Borobudur a few miles away — a cluster of tall, sharp-edged temples to the Trimurti, walls carved with the Ramayana.

works.title: Angkor Wat
works.desc: The largest religious monument on earth. Built as a Hindu temple to Vishnu, converted to Buddhist use, and never fully abandoned.

works.title: Bagan
works.desc: More than 2,000 Buddhist temples and stupas on a plain by the Irrawaddy. At dawn hot air balloons drift over a landscape of brick towers.

works.title: Wat Phra Kaew
works.desc: The royal temple inside the Grand Palace, home to the Emerald Buddha — carved from a single block of jade, robed differently for each season.

works.title: Nan Madol
works.desc: A city of 92 artificial islets built from hexagonal basalt logs, laid across a lagoon. The "Venice of the Pacific" — and a place no one has fully explained.

works.title: Moai
works.desc: Nearly 900 stone figures, carved and moved miles from the quarry. They face inland — watching over the villages, not out to sea.

works.title: Asmat Carving
works.desc: Bisj poles and shields carved from mangrove with sharpened bone. Each pole commemorates a death and demands it be avenged.

works.title: Māori Wharenui
works.desc: A carved meeting house represents an ancestor's body — ridgepole as spine, rafters as ribs. Every panel tells a story in whakairo carving.

works.title: Tā Moko
works.desc: Tattoos chiseled (not punctured) into the skin, carrying genealogy and rank. Each design is unique to the person who wears it.

works.title: Ubirr Rock Art
works.meta: Aboriginal Australian · up to 20,000 years old · Kakadu
works.desc: One of the oldest continuous art traditions on earth. Later paintings show fish anatomy so precisely that species are still identifiable.
